passage:
Text 1
Scholars examining early modern European trade networks have argued that merchant credit systems—not the volume of physical commodity exchange—were the true engine of commercial expansion. Credit instruments such as bills of exchange allowed merchants to settle debts across vast distances without the movement of coin, reducing transaction costs and enabling deals that would otherwise be impossible. Critics, however, contend that credit systems amplified rather than generated commercial growth: absent rising underlying demand for goods, credit instruments would circulate without effect.

Text 2
Researcher Valeria Moreno compiled the following data from Venetian trade records, 1450–1550:

| Decade | Bill of Exchange Volume (index) | Commodity Trade Volume (index) | Credit-to-Trade Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1450s | 100 | 100 | 1.00 |
| 1470s | 148 | 112 | 1.32 |
| 1490s | 189 | 121 | 1.56 |
| 1510s | 201 | 118 | 1.70 |
| 1530s | 198 | 107 | 1.85 |
| 1550s | 176 | 98 | 1.80 |
stem: Which claim, if true, would most effectively resolve the tension between the two positions described in Text 1 without contradicting any data in Text 2?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Credit instruments sustained commercial networks even as physical trade contracted

choices.label: B
choices.text: Credit volume growth preceded commodity growth, indicating credit as a causal driver

choices.label: C
choices.text: Credit amplified trade when demand was rising but maintained networks when demand fell

choices.label: D
choices.text: Commodity trade decline after 1530 triggered the eventual contraction of credit volume

explanation: The tension in Text 1 is between: (1) the scholars' claim that credit systems were the 'true engine' of expansion (credit drives trade), and (2) the critics' claim that credit only 'amplified' growth that underlying demand was already generating (trade drives credit). The data in Text 2 show three distinct phases: during 1450–1510, both credit and commodity volume rise, but credit rises faster (ratio increases from 1.00 to 1.70); from 1510 to 1530, commodity volume actually falls (118→107) while credit continues rising; from 1530 onward, both decline, with the credit-to-trade ratio still rising slightly (1.85 vs. 1.80 in 1550s). Choice C resolves the tension by synthesizing both positions: during the expansion phase (when demand was rising), credit amplified trade (consistent with the critics' 'amplification' view), but when demand contracted after the 1510s, credit instruments continued functioning and even increased relative to trade (consistent with the scholars' view that credit had its own independent role). This resolution honors both claims by assigning each validity in a different phase, without contradicting any data point. No distractor achieves this dual reconciliation.

distractorRationale.A: This choice is consistent with the data (credit-to-trade ratio rises even as commodity trade falls after 1510) but it only supports the scholars' position, not the critics'. It does not resolve the tension—it simply picks a side. Students who read only the declining commodity volume data may select this without recognizing it fails to reconcile both positions.
distractorRationale.B: The data show credit volume grew faster than commodity volume, but 'preceded' implies a temporal priority that the decadal data cannot establish—both indexes start at 100 in the 1450s. Moreover, even if credit preceded commodity growth, this would support one side of the debate (scholars), not resolve the tension with the critics' view. This distractor appeals to students who confuse correlation magnitude with causal priority.
distractorRationale.D: The data do show that commodity trade declined after the 1510s peak (121→118→107→98) and that credit volume also eventually declined (201→198→176). But this choice implies commodity decline caused credit contraction—this supports the critics (trade drives credit) and contradicts any independent role for credit. Furthermore, the credit-to-trade ratio continued rising through the 1530s despite commodity decline, which this causal direction cannot account for without further qualification.
